paragraphs.body: Dear Coach, I wish you could see the people on your team as more than just runners who can score points in conference. I wish you could see the natural talent that I have for running. I wish you could see how much work and effort I put into running for the past 13 years. I wish you could see how passionate I am for running. I wish you could see the complete and utter hatred I have for throwing. I wish I could understand why you ripped me away from the sport that meant so much to me. I wish I understood why you compared one of my worst times to other girls' best. I wish I understood why you said with my times I would never travel and then continue traveling girls who constantly ran slower than me. I wish I could understand why you put me through fall training if I was never going to run competitively. I wish I knew why you have been recruiting me so heavily the past 2 ½ years if I was never going to run. I wish you would stop telling me to smile more while I’m going through one of the worst periods of my life because of you! I wish you would let me run again.

annotations.text: I wish you would stop telling me to smile more
annotations.comment: For real? He actually says this?

paragraphs.body: I wonder if my coach is aware of a recent study out of NYU. Which talks about all the great effects that dopamine releases from running have included in my life, and the lives of many others: memory, motivation, and learning.The NYU research study shows that ”In addition, past research has tied exercise-driven boosts in the dopamine-triggering chemical called brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and in dopamine levels to improvements in learning and memory. ” The NYU research study demonstrates that without exercises like running, humans are deprived of the chemical reaction that occurs in your brain When exercising that helps improve learning and memory. The researchers in the NYU study also explain that without this dopamine release, it is easier for people to lose motor skills and it’s easier to develop depression. Than the NYU researchers later go onto say “Benefits of exercise on brain health are well-established. These include better memory, happier mood, decreased anxiety, and improved motor performance. ” So not only is my coach taking one of the most important and fun aspects of my life, but he could be hindering my motor skills as well as deteriorating my mental health.

annotations.text: reat effects that dopamine releases from running have included in my life, and the lives of many others: memory, motivation, and learning.

paragraphs.body: Growing up I did lots of sports, the main ones that I stuck with were basketball, cheer, and track. From ages 6 to now if you asked any what my favorite sport is everyone would have the same answer, “track. ” There were lots of things that I enjoyed about track that I did not get from anywhere else. The first one would be that it was a sport that I did not have to think about at all. With basketball you have to think about what plays you're doing and basically how to always be one step ahead. And although I don’t think cheer is really a sport, there were a lot of things I had to think about in cheer too, I had to know all the cheers and dances as well as really think about my technique when it came to stunting and tumbling. But with track I did not have to think about anything. Track was just running as fast as you can and then it was over. Another thing I enjoyed about track is that what you put into it is how much you got back. This means that no one can cheat in track, how hard you work is going to show up on the track during race day. And Finally this one might be a little shallow but, another thing that I liked about track is how effortlessly the sport came to me. It was something that I was very good at and it made me feel really good about myself and all the hard work I put in to get to this point. Nothing else is life has ever come that easily to me.

annotations.text: Track was just running as fast as you can and then it was over. Another thing I enjoyed about track is that what you put into it is how much you got back. This means that no one can cheat in track, how hard you work is going to show up on the track during race day. And Finally this one might be a little shallow but, another thing that I liked about track is how effortlessly the sport came to me. It was something that I was very good at and it made me feel really good about myself and all the hard work I put in to get to this point.
annotations.comment: Great details :) I love the authenticity of your voice, too

paragraphs.body: There’s also nothing like the feeling when you are about to run a race. It all starts for me at least a few days before. The routine took place a few nights before, to prepare me for track meet. First, I needed to find the heat sheets and look at every girl who was in my heat, and every time they had ever run. A lot of coaches say not to do this because it usually stresses competitors out, but to me it gave me a sense of peace knowing exactly who was in my heat. Not that it mattered though because in track anything can happen. Someone can be having a really good day and run a time they never thought was possible, or somebody could be having a really bad day and not put out the times that everyone is expecting from them. Now, the routine skips over to the night before race day, this part for me is really important and it starts when I’m laying in bed waiting to fall asleep but I can’t because I’m more excited for the meet. And you have to lay down and visualize the race, and what to do to execute and win. Next part of my routine for race day is the morning before, this part changed between middle school to high school. I always had to have the same breakfast every morning on race day. It was a bowl of oatmeal with an alani nu energy drink. After I ate breakfast and sipped my energy drink it was time for one of my favorite parts, the getting ready process.

annotations.comment: I love the repetition of "I wish." This is a surprising and highly charged intro. Well done.
annotations.context_left: ts for me at least a few days before. The routine took place a few nights before, to prepare me for

paragraphs.body: The motto I had for running was look good, feel good, run good. While most people think of Sha’carri Richardson when people say “look good, feel good, run good,” I think of Sydney McLaughlin. I think of her because she came on the track seen during my early days of track despite only being 7 years older than me. She was the first girl I saw to have earring stack, rings brackets, slick backs, and her nails done during competition. This would be the time when I experimented with new hairstyles and put on my makeup while blasting my favorite music at the time. At this point I used so much time and it was time to go. No matter how far away the meet was I needed my headphones. I always listened to music on the bus. I am a very talkative person but before I run the 100 meter dash I would barely say a word. This is when I was the most focused. Now we are about an hour before a race. I am warming up and doing my run outs. (These are when you model the beginning of the race and run as fast as you can for about 30 meters. Now I’m about 10 minutes before the race starts and I’m lined up.

annotations.text: Now I’m about 10 minutes before the race starts and I’m lined up
annotations.comment: What an interesting return to the "process" of preparing for a race.

annotations.text: he motto I had for running was look good, feel good, run good. While most people think of Sha’carri Richardson when people say “look good, feel good, run good,” I think of Sydney McLaughlin.

paragraphs.body: Finally it’s my turn to run. When I set up my blocks I don't use my feet to measure out my blocks like most people do. Instead I memorized my numbers and would just put them in that setting. Then I would take a small runout in my blocks before the official blows this whistle and says “on your marks” This is when I finally get into my blocks and my heart's beating really fast but I am so excited, and no feeling compares to the one that’s happening at this moment. “set” and then the gun goes off and I don't hear anything. Up to the collegiate level 9/10 I did not see anyone, I was also so focused that I never heard anyone either. I was just running as fast as I could. Finally I crossed that line(it was Usually a win) I looked at the clock and then walked off the track and then my talkative personality came back, and that’s the perfect description for my races and why nothing else can compare to that.

annotations.comment: This paragraph is also chock-full of excellent details and a compelling voice.
annotations.context_left: ack, and that’s the perfect description for my races and why nothing else can compare to that.

annotations.comment: Excellent voice and details throughout this essay. I love your intro paragraph, too. I wonder, though, what this essay's central focusing idea is? While the essay does a fabulous job of recreating your process and experience of track (and its connection to your life and identity), I'm not yet quite sure how the essay connects to, and expands on, your research and summary. Nice work, though :) It's fun to read.
